name: Slow Motion
description: Selected gameplay systems run at a reduced simulation scale while input, UI, or audio may remain responsive.
design_notes: Slow motion is most useful when the scope is explicit: which gameplay_clock_id is scaled, which ui_clock_id remains unscaled, how animation time scale and physics step behavior are handled, and whether multiplayer authority permits the effect. Without those boundaries it can quietly break input buffering, projectile travel, and camera feedback.
design_purpose: Let players perceive and react to dense action that would otherwise be unreadable.
Create a cinematic reward for skillful dodges, shots, crashes, or near misses.
Support puzzle or traversal timing by temporarily widening decision windows.
player_fantasy: The player feels intensely focused as the world slows while their intent remains sharp.

parameters.name: simulation_scale_multiplier
parameters.description: Multiplier applied to the affected gameplay clock while the slow motion effect is active.

parameters.name: effect_window_ms
parameters.description: Milliseconds the dilation remains active before blending back to normal simulation pace.

parameters.name: entry_blend_ms
parameters.description: Blend window used when entering the slowed state so cameras, animation, and audio do not snap.

parameters.name: physics_step_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides how fixed timestep simulation changes during the dilation effect.

parameters.name: unscaled_channel_mask
parameters.description: Systems that continue using unscaled_time so menus, prompts, and accessibility settings remain responsive.

edge_cases: Projectiles, hitboxes, particles, and animation notifies run on different clocks during the slowed window.
The effect begins while a cooldown, rhythm judgment, or timed challenge is using another clock source.
A multiplayer client predicts slow motion locally even though the server rejects the trigger.
Physics substepping changes collision results when the simulation scale becomes extremely low.
common_bugs: UI prompts and input buffering become sluggish because they were accidentally attached to the scaled gameplay clock.
Audio pitch drops for dialogue or accessibility cues that should remain intelligible.
Animation events fire at scaled playback while weapon traces still use unscaled frame deltas.
The game exits slow motion but fixed timestep settings are not restored for later scenes.
balancing_notes: Lower simulation scale gives dramatic readability but can trivialize aiming, dodging, and puzzle execution.
Limit availability through resource cost, trigger rarity, or encounter-specific rules if slow motion solves too many threats.
Camera zoom, chromatic effects, and audio filtering should support readability without overwhelming the player.
accessibility_notes: Allow players to reduce or disable motion distortion, heavy screen effects, and audio pitch shifts during slow motion.
Consider optional assist settings that provide slower reaction windows without tying them to flashy camera effects.

implementation_notes.unity: Use Time.timeScale only for the channels that can safely follow global dilation, and keep UI, input buffering, and menu logic on unscaledDeltaTime. If Time.fixedDeltaTime is adjusted, restore it through a scoped clock manager and audit Animator update modes, particle simulation, and Cinemachine impulses.
implementation_notes.godot: Engine.time_scale can drive broad dilation, but CanvasLayer UI, audio cues, and pause menus should read an unscaled clock. For physics-heavy games, define a physics_step_policy_id so CharacterBody and RigidBody behavior remains stable when the scale multiplier changes.
implementation_notes.unreal: Global Time Dilation, Custom Time Dilation, and physics substepping can be combined, but authority should live in a gameplay component that records trigger_id, scale, and expiry timestamp. Keep UMG and accessibility widgets on real time and replicate only approved dilation states.
implementation_notes.web: Maintain separate gameplay_clock_id and ui_clock_id values in the main loop. Phaser or custom canvas code should integrate simulation with scaled delta, render UI with real delta, and clamp accumulated frame deltas after tab focus changes.

example_games.title: Max Payne
example_games.note: Uses slow motion as a core action readability and style mechanic.

example_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild
example_games.note: Uses temporary slow motion during some aerial aiming moments.
